_meta.description: Functional decomposition, use case modelling, acceptance criteria, and functional specification techniques.

mcq.q: What is the main purpose of identifying functional requirements in a PL-600 engagement?
options: To collect technical implementation ideas
To define what the solution must do to support business processes and user tasks
To decide infrastructure sizing
To replace fit/gap analysis
mcq.explanation: Functional requirements describe the behaviors, capabilities, and actions the solution must provide to support business processes and user outcomes.
wrongExplanations: Implementation ideas may come later, but they are not the definition of functional requirements.
Infrastructure sizing is a separate architectural concern.
Fit/gap analysis still compares requirements to capabilities after they are identified.

msRef.label: Work with requirements for Microsoft Power Platform and Dynamics 365

mcq.q: Which statement is the best example of a functional requirement?
options: The system should feel modern
Managers must be able to approve discount exceptions from a mobile device
The app should load quickly
Customer data must remain in a specific geography
mcq.explanation: A functional requirement defines something the solution must allow a user to do. Mobile approval capability is a concrete business function.
wrongExplanations: This is vague and subjective.
This is mainly a non-functional performance expectation.
This is primarily a data residency constraint.

msRef.label: Work with requirements for Microsoft Power Platform and Dynamics 365

mcq.q: What is the strongest reason to decompose a broad functional requirement into smaller parts?
options: To make documents look more detailed
To expose distinct capabilities, rules, dependencies, and validation conditions
To avoid speaking with stakeholders again
To guarantee the same sprint size
mcq.explanation: Decomposition turns broad needs into clearer functional capabilities that can be prioritized, validated, and compared against solution options.
wrongExplanations: More detail is not the goal by itself.
Stakeholder validation is still needed after decomposition.
Sprint size is not the main architectural reason to decompose.

msRef.label: Define your requirements for your Dynamics 365 solution

mcq.q: A stakeholder says, "We need better service handling." What is the best next step to identify the functional requirement?
options: Build a service dashboard prototype immediately
Ask what users need to do differently, which business steps must be supported, and what successful handling looks like
Estimate custom tables for service handling
Convert it into a single backlog item called Improve service
mcq.explanation: The architect should uncover the user actions, process steps, and intended outcomes that define the real functional need.
wrongExplanations: Prototyping before clarifying the requirement can lock the team into the wrong path.
Technical estimates are premature.
That keeps the requirement vague.

msRef.label: Discover customer needs as a Solution Architect for Dynamics 365 and Microsoft Power Platform

mcq.q: Which requirement statement is written best for functional analysis?
options: Create three forms and two custom tables
As a case worker, I need to assign a case to a specialist with a due date so that urgent issues are tracked to completion
Users should be happier
The solution should be scalable
mcq.explanation: This statement clearly identifies actor, action, and business purpose, which makes it useful for functional analysis and design.
wrongExplanations: This is implementation detail rather than a functional requirement statement.
This is too vague to analyze functionally.
This is non-functional rather than functional.

msRef.label: Work with requirements for Microsoft Power Platform and Dynamics 365

mcq.q: What is the main value of use case modelling when identifying functional requirements?
options: It replaces workshop facilitation
It shows how actors interact with the solution across scenarios and helps reveal missing steps or conditions
It focuses only on database schema
It guarantees no customization will be needed
mcq.explanation: Use case modelling helps visualize who interacts with the solution, what they need to do, and where functional gaps or alternate flows may exist.
wrongExplanations: Workshops are still needed to discover and validate requirements.
Use case modelling is broader than schema design.
It does not guarantee standard fit.

msRef.label: Workshops for Dynamics 365 implementation projects

mcq.q: Which question best helps identify hidden functional requirements?
options: Which color theme should the app use?
What must users be able to create, update, review, approve, or escalate that they cannot do effectively today?
Which icon set do managers prefer?
How many developers are on the project?
mcq.explanation: This question focuses on the actual business actions users need to perform, which is where hidden functional needs usually surface.
wrongExplanations: Theme choice is not a core functional discovery question.
Icon preference does not identify business function.
Team staffing does not define user needs.

msRef.label: Work with requirements for Microsoft Power Platform and Dynamics 365

mcq.q: What is the strongest reason to map functional requirements to business processes?
options: To make the requirement list longer
To show where each requirement fits in the end-to-end flow and expose missing or duplicate behavior
To avoid documenting assumptions
To remove the need for use cases
mcq.explanation: Mapping requirements to processes helps the team understand where functions are needed and whether the full business scenario is supported.
wrongExplanations: More text is not the goal.
Assumptions may still need to be documented.
Use cases and process mapping can complement each other.

msRef.label: Define your requirements for your Dynamics 365 solution

mcq.q: A requirement says, "Integrate with finance." Why is this not yet a usable functional requirement?
options: Because integrations are non-functional only
Because it does not explain which business events, data, actions, and outcomes the solution must support
Because finance systems cannot be integrated
Because it is already detailed enough
mcq.explanation: The statement is too broad. A usable functional requirement must clarify what business behavior the integration enables and under what conditions.
wrongExplanations: Integrations often support functional behavior and are not purely non-functional.
Finance systems can absolutely be integrated depending on context.
It is too vague for design.

msRef.label: Discover customer needs as a Solution Architect for Dynamics 365 and Microsoft Power Platform

mcq.q: What is the primary role of acceptance criteria in functional requirement work?
options: To define whether the functional behavior has been specified clearly enough to test and accept
To replace user stories
To describe infrastructure topology
To estimate project budget
mcq.explanation: Acceptance criteria make functional expectations testable and help verify whether the requirement has been met.
wrongExplanations: User stories and acceptance criteria serve different purposes.
Infrastructure topology is unrelated.
Acceptance criteria do not estimate budget.

msRef.label: Work with requirements for Microsoft Power Platform and Dynamics 365

mcq.q: Which is the best example of acceptance criteria for a functional requirement?
options: Managers should like the approval process
A discount request can be submitted only when amount, reason, and approver are present, and an approver receives notification after submission
The solution should be intuitive
The app should be modern
mcq.explanation: This defines concrete functional conditions and expected system behavior, making the requirement testable.
wrongExplanations: This is subjective and not testable enough.
This is too vague and more usability-oriented.
This is aspirational, not acceptance logic.

msRef.label: Work with requirements for Microsoft Power Platform and Dynamics 365

mcq.q: Why should alternate flows and exception paths be considered when identifying functional requirements?
options: Because they often reveal missing behavior the core happy path does not cover
Because they reduce the need for validation
Because only exception paths matter in design
Because they guarantee the need for customization
mcq.explanation: Functional completeness depends on more than the ideal path. Alternate and exception flows often reveal missing requirements and operational realities.
wrongExplanations: Validation is still required.
Happy paths still matter too.
Exception handling does not automatically force customization.

msRef.label: Workshops for Dynamics 365 implementation projects

mcq.q: Which statement best reflects good functional specification technique?
options: Document only the intended screens
Describe actors, triggers, behavior, rules, data needed, and outcomes clearly enough to support design and validation
Record stakeholder comments without interpretation
Focus only on database entities
mcq.explanation: A useful functional specification captures the behavior, rules, data, and context required for design and testing.
wrongExplanations: Screen descriptions alone are too narrow.
Raw comments often preserve ambiguity.
Database entities alone do not define functional behavior.

msRef.label: Define your requirements for your Dynamics 365 solution

mcq.q: A stakeholder demands a custom console for agents. Which architect question is best for uncovering the underlying functional need?
options: Which framework should be used for the console?
What actions do agents need to perform in one place that they cannot perform efficiently today?
How many tabs should the console have?
Can we start a prototype tomorrow?
mcq.explanation: The architect should translate the requested interface into the underlying user actions and capability needs.
wrongExplanations: This jumps to implementation.
Tab count is premature.
A prototype before requirement clarity is risky.

msRef.label: Discover customer needs as a Solution Architect for Dynamics 365 and Microsoft Power Platform

mcq.q: What is the best reason to identify dependencies between functional requirements?
options: To make the backlog more complex
To understand sequencing, design impact, and whether one capability depends on another existing first
To avoid stakeholder discussion
To reduce documentation effort
mcq.explanation: Dependencies affect delivery order, design choices, and how capability groups should be planned.
wrongExplanations: Complexity is not the goal.
Stakeholder discussion is still needed.
Dependency analysis usually requires more deliberate documentation, not less.

msRef.label: Plan a Dynamics 365 implementation strategy

mcq.q: Which requirement statement is most clearly functional rather than non-functional?
options: The system must support 99.9% availability
Sales reps must be able to convert a qualified lead into an opportunity
The app must open within three seconds
Data must remain in the EU
mcq.explanation: This describes a business action the system must support, which is the core of a functional requirement.
wrongExplanations: This is reliability-oriented and non-functional.
This is performance-oriented and non-functional.
This is a residency/compliance constraint.

msRef.label: Work with requirements for Microsoft Power Platform and Dynamics 365

mcq.q: What is the strongest reason to group functional requirements by role or task?
options: To make documentation look organized
To design applications and experiences around how different users actually work
To avoid business process analysis
To eliminate security design
mcq.explanation: Grouping by role or task helps ensure the solution supports real working patterns rather than a generic undifferentiated feature list.
wrongExplanations: Organization alone is not the main value.
Business process analysis is still needed.
Security design remains necessary.

msRef.label: Study guide for Exam PL-600: Microsoft Power Platform Solution Architect

mcq.q: Why should functional requirements be reviewed against standard Dynamics 365 capabilities before customization is recommended?
options: Because standard capability may already satisfy the required function with less complexity
Because customization is never allowed
Because requirements should be rewritten as code first
Because only ISV products can close gaps
mcq.explanation: The architect should compare functional needs to standard capabilities first, because standard fit may reduce cost, complexity, and maintenance burden.
wrongExplanations: Customization can be valid when justified.
Requirements should not be rewritten as code first.
ISV products are one option, not the only one.

msRef.label: Perform fit gap analysis

mcq.q: Which statement best shows that a functional requirement is still too vague?
options: Users must be able to submit a service request with category, priority, and attachments
Managers should have better visibility
Approvers must receive a notification when a request is submitted
Agents must be able to reassign a case with a reason
mcq.explanation: Better visibility is too broad and needs refinement into specific user actions, data needs, or outcomes.
wrongExplanations: This is already functionally concrete.
This is a specific functional behavior.
This is also specific and testable.

msRef.label: Work with requirements for Microsoft Power Platform and Dynamics 365

mcq.q: What is the best functional analysis response when different regions want different approval flows?
options: Capture all differences as mandatory by default
Analyze which variations are truly required and which can be standardized into a common functional model
Ignore the differences until UAT
Ask developers to choose one flow
mcq.explanation: The architect should distinguish justified variation from legacy inconsistency so the functional model is not unnecessarily complex.
wrongExplanations: That often preserves unnecessary complexity.
UAT is too late.
This is not purely a developer decision.

msRef.label: Perform fit gap analysis

mcq.q: Why are triggers and outcomes important in a functional specification?
options: They define when the function starts and what result it must produce
They replace process mapping
They are only needed for custom code
They matter only for integration stories
mcq.explanation: A clear functional specification should explain what starts the behavior and what the expected result is.
wrongExplanations: Process mapping can still add context.
They matter for functional behavior generally, not only custom code.
They matter beyond integration scenarios.

msRef.label: Define your requirements for your Dynamics 365 solution

tf.q: True or False: Functional requirements describe what users or processes must be able to do in the solution.
tf.explanation: That is the core meaning of functional requirements in Microsoft’s requirements guidance. :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}

msRef.label: Work with requirements for Microsoft Power Platform and Dynamics 365

tf.q: True or False: Acceptance criteria help determine whether a functional requirement has been implemented correctly enough to validate and accept.
tf.explanation: Acceptance criteria make requirements testable and support validation of expected behavior.

msRef.label: Work with requirements for Microsoft Power Platform and Dynamics 365

tf.q: True or False: Use case modelling is useless once workshops have been completed.
tf.explanation: Use case modelling remains useful after workshops because it helps structure and validate what was learned across scenarios and actors.

msRef.label: Workshops for Dynamics 365 implementation projects

tf.q: True or False: A requirement written only as a proposed screen or custom component is usually not strong enough as a functional requirement.
tf.explanation: A proposed screen or component may be part of a solution idea, but it does not fully express the business function, rules, triggers, and outcomes needed for proper requirement analysis.

msRef.label: Discover customer needs as a Solution Architect for Dynamics 365 and Microsoft Power Platform

tf.q: True or False: Alternate and exception flows can reveal missing functional requirements that a happy-path-only view would miss.
tf.explanation: That is one of the practical reasons to model scenarios beyond the happy path.

msRef.label: Workshops for Dynamics 365 implementation projects

dragdrop.instruction: Match each example to the correct category.
items: As a sales rep, I need to convert a qualified lead to an opportunity so that the pipeline is tracked correctly
A discount request can be submitted only when amount, reason, and approver are present
The system must support case creation within 3 seconds
dragdrop.explanation: Functional work is stronger when the team can distinguish functional capability, acceptance logic, vague statements, and non-functional expectations.

msRef.label: Work with requirements for Microsoft Power Platform and Dynamics 365

dragdrop.instruction: Match each activity to the main purpose.
items: Map a requirement to a business process
zones: Understand actor interactions and scenario flow
Show where the function fits in end-to-end operations

correctMapping.Model a use case: Understand actor interactions and scenario flow
correctMapping.Map a requirement to a business process: Show where the function fits in end-to-end operations

dragdrop.explanation: These activities support different parts of strong functional requirement work: clarity, scenario understanding, validation, and process context.

msRef.label: Define your requirements for your Dynamics 365 solution

dragdrop.instruction: Match each architect concern to the most relevant question.
zones: What else must this user do that is not currently documented?
Under what conditions is this action allowed or blocked?
What happens when the standard path fails or the data is incomplete?
What business outcome are you trying to achieve rather than what tool do you want built?

correctMapping.Hidden user task: What else must this user do that is not currently documented?
correctMapping.Unclear business rule: Under what conditions is this action allowed or blocked?
correctMapping.Missing exception flow: What happens when the standard path fails or the data is incomplete?
correctMapping.Premature solution idea: What business outcome are you trying to achieve rather than what tool do you want built?

dragdrop.explanation: Good architect questioning helps surface hidden tasks, rules, exception handling, and business intent behind proposed solutions.

msRef.label: Discover customer needs as a Solution Architect for Dynamics 365 and Microsoft Power Platform

casestudy.title: Case Study - Functional Requirements Hidden Inside a Solution Request
casestudy.scenario: A customer wants a new service workspace for handling warranty claims. During workshops, managers ask for a custom console, dashboards, quick actions, and automated escalations. Frontline agents explain that the real problems are incomplete claim information, inconsistent approval thresholds, difficulty finding related warranty history, and confusion about when claims must be escalated. Regional teams also handle exception claims differently. The initial notes mix desired screens, missing business rules, functional gaps, and a few performance expectations.

msRef.label: Workshops for Dynamics 365 implementation projects

questions.q: What should the solution architect do first?
options: Approve the custom console direction and start wireframes
Separate solution ideas from user actions, rules, and outcomes so the true functional requirements can be defined
Ignore the frontline agent input because managers own the budget
Convert every request into a custom development item
questions.explanation: The first job is to separate proposed implementation from the actual behaviors, rules, and outcomes that the solution must support.
wrongExplanations: That would anchor the team on a solution before the requirement is clear.
Frontline operational detail is critical for functional analysis.
That would preserve ambiguity and likely over-customize.

msRef.label: Discover customer needs as a Solution Architect for Dynamics 365 and Microsoft Power Platform

questions.q: Which issue most clearly points to a missing functional requirement?
options: The app should be fast
Agents struggle to find related warranty history during claim handling
Data must stay in one geography
Managers prefer a single screen
questions.explanation: Difficulty finding warranty history points to a missing user capability the solution must support during the claim-handling process.
wrongExplanations: This is a non-functional performance concern.
This is a residency constraint rather than a functional capability.
This is closer to a UX preference than a clearly defined functional requirement.

msRef.label: Work with requirements for Microsoft Power Platform and Dynamics 365

questions.q: What is the best way to refine the inconsistent approval thresholds problem?
options: Capture a single requirement saying approvals differ
Define the specific approval triggers, threshold rules, approver roles, and exception conditions by scenario
Leave it for UAT
Let developers infer the rules from sample data
questions.explanation: Approval logic is functional behavior driven by explicit business rules. Those rules must be clarified by scenario to become design-ready.
wrongExplanations: That is still too vague.
UAT is too late to discover core business rules.
Developers should not infer business rules.

msRef.label: Define your requirements for your Dynamics 365 solution

questions.q: Which acceptance criterion is strongest for the claim escalation function?
options: Escalations should feel easy
A claim must be escalated automatically when claim value exceeds the approved threshold or required warranty evidence is missing
Managers need visibility into escalations
The app should support urgent work
questions.explanation: This is specific, rule-based, and testable, which makes it strong acceptance logic for a functional requirement.
wrongExplanations: This is subjective and not testable enough.
This is a broader functional need, not acceptance logic.
This is too vague.

msRef.label: Work with requirements for Microsoft Power Platform and Dynamics 365

questions.q: What should the architect do about regional exception-claim differences?
options: Capture every variation as mandatory by default
Analyze which exception flows are truly required and which can be standardized into the core process
Ignore them because they affect only edge cases
Design a separate application for each region immediately
questions.explanation: The architect should evaluate whether the variations are justified business needs or legacy inconsistency before carrying them into the functional model.
wrongExplanations: That often creates unnecessary complexity.
Edge cases can still be business-critical.
Separate applications are a premature and extreme response.

msRef.label: Perform fit gap analysis

questions.q: Which statement from the workshop is most clearly a premature solution idea rather than a functional requirement?
options: Agents need faster access to related warranty history
Claims must escalate when evidence is missing
We need a custom console
Approvals depend on claim value and region
questions.explanation: A custom console is a proposed implementation. The underlying requirement is about what users need to do and why.
wrongExplanations: This is a user capability need.
This is a functional business rule.
This is also a functional rule statement.

msRef.label: Discover customer needs as a Solution Architect for Dynamics 365 and Microsoft Power Platform

questions.q: What is the best final output from this stage of analysis?
options: A list of requested screens only
A refined set of actor-based functional requirements, rules, exception flows, and acceptance criteria mapped to the warranty-claim process
A developer-only task list
A branding guide for the service workspace
questions.explanation: That output gives the team a usable functional model for design, validation, fit-gap work, and prioritization.
wrongExplanations: Screens alone are too narrow.
A developer-only list loses business context.
Branding does not solve the functional definition problem.

msRef.label: Define your requirements for your Dynamics 365 solution
